summary: Stancil argues that the modern data stack lacks a true “single pane of glass,” and that multiple tool categories are converging to claim that central control role. This competitive rush, driven by venture funding expectations, makes fragmentation worse until the market forces a single winner.
money_quotes: But to get there, we also have to agree on who should provide that pane of glass—and right now, there’s nothing close to a consensus on that question.
The biggest looming battle, however, will be over a different territory: The brain—or operating system—of the data stack.
Generally speaking, if we can’t get something right in that many tries, the issue probably isn’t some tweak that we’ll fix on the next one.
The issue is systemic—and each new effort to make it better actually makes the fragmentation worse.
Because by 2032, someone will own it. But there can only be one unifier—there can only be one single source of truth, one single pane of glass—and not everyone can be king.
key_insight: The data stack’s chaos is less a tooling problem than a market structure problem, and VC-driven competition will force a single “control tower” winner even as it intensifies fragmentation in the meantime.
